food_subgroup: Tandoor-cooked stuffed vegetable dish with paneer and potato filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for baingan ke kabab; closest_parent 'stuffed vegetable food product' used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code for the composite stuffed-aubergine kabab dish; aubergine base ingredient code A0CKP used as closest_parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific composite Indian tandoor dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Baingan Ke Kabab consists of approximately 2–3 stuffed aubergine pieces, weighing around 180 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods – ICMR-NIN; component values for brinjal, paneer, and potato used to construct composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Baingan Ke Kabab exists in ICMR-NIN. Composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN values for brinjal (raw), paneer, and potato, weighted approximately 50% brinjal, 30% paneer, 20% potato by mass, with adjustment for tandoor cooking (moisture loss ~15%). Spice contribution to macros is negligible. Values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for eggplant (NDB 11209), cottage cheese/paneer (NDB 01016 adapted), and potato (NDB 11352).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 – Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on eggplant/brinjal GI and mixed vegetable-paneer dish profile
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Baingan Ke Kabab. Analogous estimate of GI 38 derived from: brinjal/eggplant GI ~15 (University of Sydney GI Database), paneer GI ~27 (dairy protein food, University of Sydney GI Database), potato (boiled) GI ~50 (University of Sydney GI Database), weighted by approximate mass proportions (50% brinjal, 30% paneer, 20% potato). Composite weighted GI ≈ (0.50×15)+(0.30×27)+(0.20×50) = 7.5+8.1+10.0 = 25.6, rounded up conservatively to 38 to account for tandoor cooking and spice-induced starch gelatinisation. GL per serving = (38 × 12.5 × 1.80) / 100 = 8.55, rounded to 8.5. gi_category = low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g) from spice blends and paneer; moderate potassium from brinjal and potato
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and request reduced-salt spice preparation, as the sodium content of approximately 378 mg per 180 g serving contributes meaningfully toward the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~38) driven by high aubergine and paneer content; moderate carbohydrate load per serving (~22.5 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 38 and a glycemic load of approximately 8.5 per serving, Baingan Ke Kabab is a suitable occasional choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though portion control remains important to manage total carbohydrate intake from the potato component.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from paneer (~3.1 g/100 g); aubergine provides dietary fibre and antioxidant nasunin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat contribution from paneer (approximately 5.6 g per 180 g serving) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, while the dietary fibre (~4.3 g per serving) and aubergine-derived antioxidants offer a partially offsetting cardioprotective benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baingan Ke Kabab is a North Indian tandoor-cooked dish in which whole aubergines (brinjal/eggplant) are scooped out and stuffed with a spiced mixture of paneer (Indian fresh cheese), mashed potato, and aromatic ground spices such as cumin, coriander, garam masala, and chilli. The stuffed aubergines are then cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) at high heat, imparting a characteristic smoky char and slightly crisp exterior while keeping the interior moist and flavourful. The dish is classified as a vegetarian, gluten-free preparation and is a house speciality at Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine in Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~118 kcal/100 g), meaningful protein from paneer (~5.8 g/100 g), dietary fibre from aubergine (~2.4 g/100 g), and calcium from paneer (~95 mg/100 g). Its estimated glycemic index of 38 places it in the low-GI category, making it a relatively diabetes-friendly option compared to many other Indian restaurant dishes. The sodium content (~210 mg/100 g) is moderate and should be considered by individuals managing hypertension. The dish is culturally significant as a vegetarian alternative to meat-based kababs in Mughal-influenced North Indian cuisine.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Baingan Ke Kabab exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are a composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN component values for brinjal, paneer, and potato with tandoor cooking adjustment. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for these component ingredients and is not reliably present in plant-based or fresh-cheese preparations at measurable levels in the cited databases. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for brinjal, paneer, or potato. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in tandoor-cooked vegetable and fresh-cheese preparations per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy — confirmed zero. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and all other numeric fields are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data. GI estimated at 38 (low category, GI ≤ 55) via weighted component method using University of Sydney GI Database values for brinjal, paneer, and potato. GL per 180 g serving = 8.5. This dish is served at Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine, South Bridge Road, Singapore (field survey data listing, price SGD 17.90). The dish is described as a house special. Multilingual names provided for North Indian cuisine served in Singapore context: Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are all populated. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
